justification: All pass criteria met: Option B recommended with 4+ explicit reasons (reliability via queue persistence, open/closed for new channels, delivery service latency isolation, partial failure isolation). Trade-offs list both gains AND sacrifices for every decision. Event schema explicitly named as rigidity_point with mitigation strategy. Channel extensibility identified as flexibility_point. Implementation guidance is highly concrete with phased timeline, specific file paths, technology recommendations (SQS/Cloud Tasks), and explicit pitfalls. Small-team constraint factored throughout (managed queue over self-hosted, single worker over per-channel, Kafka/RabbitMQ explicitly rejected, no CQRS/event sourcing). Risks section includes queue infrastructure risks (poison messages, DLQ growing unnoticed).

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed implementation guidance with phased rollout, specific file paths, and concrete pitfalls — far exceeds 'actionable for Shaq' bar
Small-team constraint woven into every decision, not just acknowledged once — managed queue, single worker, deferred outbox pattern, explicit rejection of Kafka/RabbitMQ
Rigidity points go beyond just event schema — also identifies at-least-once semantics and vendor lock-in as architectural constraints
Idempotent Consumer pattern identified proactively — a critical concern for at-least-once delivery that many architects miss
Trade-offs section covers three distinct decisions with alternatives_considered for each, not just the main Option A vs B choice
Domain model with bounded contexts and context map adds strategic DDD value beyond what was required
Transactional Outbox mentioned as phase 2 possibility — while properly deferred, this adds scope creep risk for a small team. Could have been relegated to a footnote rather than a named pattern.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed implementation_guidance with phased rollout, specific file paths, and concrete pitfalls — far exceeds 'actionable next steps for Shaq'

Multiple trade-off entries (not just Option A vs B) — also covers outbox vs managed queue and single vs per-channel workers
